interactions.summary: Jiang extends Asha as responsibility to teach truth, then shifts from basic Zoroastrian ideas into historical reconstruction of Zarathustra as a Bronze Age priest-prophet.
Jiang reads or paraphrases the Gathas and explains Asha through individual choice, happiness, good thoughts, good words, good actions, and resistance to demonic violence.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang reading and interpreting quoted poetry/scripture

speaker_notes.note: Focus refs are Jiang monologue with embedded quoted Gathas; quotations are read in Jiang's voice.

claims.claim: Asha is not only self-truth; the one who sees truth has a responsibility to help others see it too.
Jiang estimates Zarathustra between 2000 and 1000 BCE and suspects the late Bronze Age because mature capital had produced slavery, debt, corruption, misery, and desire for another voice.
Zarathustra is reconstructed as a poor priest-prophet in or near northern Iran, close to a violent mining frontier connected to the Bronze Age economy.
The Gathas teach Jiang's core triad of individual responsibility, free will, and moral unity: good thoughts, good words, and good actions.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretation of quoted Gathas in this lecture.

signature_moments.moment: Asha is a choir: Ahura Mazda sings, and humans must tune themselves and help others tune.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Ahura Masta, the Monad, he's singing a song. We have to sing along with him.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The metaphor makes virtue collective without erasing individual responsibility.

signature_moments.moment: Zarathustra turns against corrupt priests who sell immortality.
signature_moments.source_phrase: They want a lot of money for helping you achieve immortality.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It casts the prophet as a rebellion against religious extraction.

signature_moments.moment: Happiness becomes evidence that one is moving along Asha.
signature_moments.source_phrase: How do you know you're doing Asha? Because you are happy.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It ties truth to felt transformation rather than external approval.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The poetic material attributed to Zarathustra that Jiang uses to explain Asha, twin spirits, and good thoughts/words/actions.

glossary_terms.term: Good thoughts, good words, good actions
glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's formulation of unified Zoroastrian virtue across mind, speech, and deed.
Rendered in ASR as Davahs; demons or false powers associated with choosing violence and the lie.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR says “Middle Ages” where context indicates Mesopotamia/Egypt/Levant as Bronze Age centers; extraction keeps the intended Bronze Age geography.
